speaker: interviewer
text: --as you pronounce it. And I'm Donna Kanter, the interviewer. Today is March 16, 1995. This is tape 3 of our interview. Let's continue with the distinction of-- you were saying that you were sent constantly throughout the year-and-a-half period to different detail.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, the thing is, I was three and a half years in ghettos and concentration camps. And it started out with the open ghetto, and then, little by little, they concentrated us. And at first it was a bigger ghetto, which was one-- one big street in our hometown, was little to begin with. And then it became smaller and smaller, until they just-- they built a camp-- the [GERMAN], and that was small with regular barracks. Because in the ghetto we were still living in people's houses that used to live there because it was a part of town. But when we got to the camp, these were actual barracks that were built specially for us. They were wooden barracks, and we slept on-- on-- on like a wooden-- a wooden bunk that was the length of the barrack-- the bunk. And everybody just slept in a row. And-- and the bunk was like in-- in the middle, so people slept head to head, head to head, and there was two rows of people. And that was the-- that was the camp. Now, from-- from the ghetto is when I finally got into the camp, when I was telling that-- that-- when I related my incident with-- with the SS. That was the entry into the camp. That was my first entry into the concentration camp or hard labor camp of Ostrowiec.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how did you feel about those experiences?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, as long as my parents were there, I was very-- I was fine. I was fine. I-- if they were there, then it was OK. It's the only explanation I have as a child. Because if your parents are there, it doesn't matter how devastating or how bad, it's OK, because your parents are there, or even though I was never really with them. I was not with my father. I-- I only slept with my mother at night, because during the day I was working and separated, so only at night when we got back to the camp after work, and I think during our meals. I don't remember how we ate our meals. All I know is that they had big vats, and they were giving us soup, so to speak, and pieces of bread. But this was good food. This was really good food, because everything got worse as time went on. As I was stating, we had these ditches dug, and they had frogs and they cooked them, and they had potatoes. And-- and on the road between the two camps in Ostrowiec, they were growing-- it's like a form of a radish, but it's not a radish. I forgot what they call it. And we used to boil that, too. So that was good. It-- it-- it filled up your tummy. So that was boiled as well in the soup. Everything went into that soup, whatever they had.

speaker: interviewer
text: Turnips?

speaker: interviewee
text: Maybe turnips, maybe turnips. If I recall that name later on in Polish, but I-- I-- I can't think of the name right now, But they were-- they were big-- big, big-- I-- I don't know what they were. Maybe in the squash family, maybe not. I don't know what. I'll-- I'll think of the name in Polish. I-- I-- I thought of it the other day, but I forgot. I didn't write it down.

speaker: interviewer
text: The men that your father worked with, what-- what kind of work were they doing?

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm not exactly sure. They-- they-- like I say, they had a steel factory in our town, and a lot of people worked. They worked for the Germans, and they helped their war effort, whatever it was that they had to do. But they worked every day.

speaker: interviewer
text: Would you talk about the song that you learned? Would this be an appropriate time for you to sing it to us, or in connection with-- year later?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I can't sing it. I don't-- I don't remember right now the words. I would have to redo it. On a different tape maybe I can-- I don't know. I-- I-- I haven't practiced that. It takes a long time. It's-- like I say, it's a four-minute song, and you have to really rehearse yourself to tell the story right. And it's got verses and song in the middle, and it's a song that tells the story of-- of the Jewish plight, so to speak. It takes place in a small town, and it probably was written during the First World War. It-- it shows a small town somewhere in Poland, where-- where the Germans invaded Poland. And they come in to a-- to a synagogue in a small town, and it happens to be Yom Kippur, and they all say in Kol Nidre. And he comes in-- the Germans come in and the Russians on-- on the other side. And-- and the Germans come in, and they say that they have hidden a-- an informer into the-- into the synagogue in the middle of Kol Nidre. And-- and the old rabbi is in awe. He-- he doesn't understand what they even mean. And here this old man finally comes forward, and he's 80 years old. And he says he is that informer, and he dies on Kiddush HaShem on Yom Kippur. And the song goes on, and Kol Nidre continues. And It's-- it's-- it's a little monologue story, and this is what I learned in that camp. And somehow I had to revive it all the time because it's involved. So [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I used to sing a lot. I used to sing a lot. Whenever everybody used to get together, and when it was, like, off time and we felt a little secure, and there were not too many Germans and they were only at the posts, and we were-- so we felt a little more free. So they used to say, sing, and I used to sing. And even though I was let into the camp, and at times I felt free. But when the Germans used to come into our camp, I remember my brothers always tell me to hide. So I used to crawl under those-- those bunks. There was a crawl space, and I used to crawl under and hide. Because as a child, you were never secure anywhere. Doesn't matter whether you worked or you didn't work. It depended on their moods, how they felt, and what transpired. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] The whole war didn't make very much sense. Nothing really made sense. Living through it, I cannot make sense of anything that I went through.

speaker: interviewer
text: So when-- when they came into the camp or-- I had to learn how to survive all the time. I had to make myself completely invisible most of the time. And I learned that very well. I learned how to make myself invisible. I used to disappear myself between people all the time.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I was never conspicuous. I learned that right away-- learned in order to survive. This is not the time to show off. There was no showing off. And this is how I found out that the people in the camp didn't-- didn't feel comfortable with me around either. Because when I used to disappear myself, so to speak, they were in jeopardy, because I used to hide amongst crowds. Wherever there was a crowd or we had to stand up and be counted somewhere, I used to be in the back or between and never up front. And not in the back either, because if they went around us I would be in the back. I had to try to be in the middle somewhere. And they didn't like it. They didn't-- the people didn't like it. But I learned how to survive. I did. And I was not-- never tall anyhow to begin with, because I'm not tall now either. And maybe I would have been a couple inches taller if not for the war maybe. I did have malnutrition. I was malnutritious during the war. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] [? Louder-- ?]

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened after the [? camps? ?] Then-- then what happened? The last job, it seemed, that you were put to work in is the Hitlerjugend camp. Then what--

speaker: interviewee
text: And in-- in the camp, I used to peel potatoes. I used to work in the kitchen. I used to-- whatever needed to be done, they put us to work. I mean, we were not idle. I don't recall day-to-day activities, but we were never really idle. We were always working in that-- in that camp in Ostrowiec. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] And like I said, these-- I-- I'm going over it very, very lightly, and I'm making light out of it. But it was a day-to-day struggle to survive daily and not to be singled out, not to be captured, [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] not to have to account for something. And to just be on the mediocre part, being part of the disappearing crowd was a daily chore, a daily chore for those-- for that year and a half in that [GERMAN] of Ostrowiec. It was a daily chore. A lot of things happened and a lot of close calls for myself. But on the general, I survived for-- for one reason. It's because I think I was-- I learned, in my own head, how to transport myself out of there. And I did. I did it daily. I-- I did it all the time. And I learned how to physically help myself to survive by a lot of little tricks that I've learned being there. And-- and sometimes I find myself even today using it. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I find when-- when crowd gathered and everybody is so curious over things, I-- I-- I-- I make sure I'm out of there, until today. I don't want to be part of a crowd. I don't. I try to be out of there. Don't want to be caught up. It-- maybe it's left over. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] I watch people, like, they see an incident on the freeway. They see a burning building. They want to stand around and watch. I want to be out of there. I'll try anything to get away from those places. I guess it's-- it's like a trigger. [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] Lots of things will-- will trigger remembrances and incidents. And I know I was young.

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened next to you and your family?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, like I said, I was really never with them from the time-- actually, from the beginning of the ghetto I was never really with anybody. I was strictly by myself. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I slept with my mother during those years, except for the ghetto when I was up on the-- on-- on the-- on the-- on the attic and-- and later on in the camp. I was working and just sleeping with my mother, and the men were separate. So I was really never together with my family once we were all separated after that bunker. So I was strictly on my own.

speaker: interviewer
text: At what point were you separated from everyone from your town to Auschwitz?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I guess it happened in 1944. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] I guess the-- the Germans were starting to lose the war, but I didn't know. I don't-- [LAUGHS] I didn't know anything. All I know is that one day, we were all marched, and we were put on those cattle wagons. Being that it was an industrial city, there were plenty of train stations, and our camp was located not too far from that because that's where the men worked.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we were put on the cattle wagons. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And-- and that's-- that's the only time where I was with my brothers and my father and my mother, in that cattle wagon. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And it was a very devastating time, very. I was little. The-- that-- that wagon was so packed with people that we-- we had no room to sit down. There was no room. You had to either stand or be stood on. It was just packed.

speaker: interviewee
text: And there was no air. I remember sitting on my brother-- my little brother's shoulder, so that through those few little openings I could catch some air. And he held me on his shoulder, because the minute I would be standing up I was in somebody's waste, in somebody's excrement. And-- and-- and-- and-- and I couldn't breathe because people were just-- there was nowhere to go. There was nowhere to-- to-- to excrete yourself, to-- to-- to-- there was nothing. It was just-- and it smelled so bad. And there was no air for me because I was shorter than everybody. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I-- I-- I don't want to exaggerate. I don't know how many days. I was told a couple days we were on these wagons. And I remember sitting on my brother's shoulders a lot just to survive, so whenever I needed air. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] It was very devastating. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] I think I was sitting on my father's shoulders. My-- my-- my father [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] and my sister, in-- before we went to one of the ghettos, contracted typhoid fever. So my-- my father was very weak. He never recovered really. He never recovered his strength, his stamina throughout the war. That's what my brothers tell me. So he couldn't really lift me even up to-- to get me to the air, but he tried. He really tried. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] And we were two days on those wagons. And mostly my little brother is the one that kept lifting me up and getting me to get some air. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] He was lucky enough to find a spot not too far away, and he-- he took care of me. He took care of me. And-- and this is how we got to Auschwitz. And we got-- we left the 1st of August. The reason I know that, I looked up-- I looked up some records, and my brothers, well, naturally, they-- they told me about it. They weren't 100% sure exactly on the dates. We weren't exactly counting calendars. And-- but I know it was August 1 that we-- we went to Auschwitz. And we got-- when we-- we got there, naturally, they separated the men from the women right away first. And then they took us into a building in Auschwitz, and I have my own child's memory of that. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] It was-- it was an eye-opener for me. I have never seen anything like it, even though I've seen so much already. I was exposed to death and killings and-- and everything else before and being myself exposed to death. But I've never seen anything like that. It was completely novel. When I watched-- when I watched those Germans walking amongst us, the women, I must have been a great observer. Because I remember they went around immaculately dressed with their boots, which is one of my nemesis. And boots really destroy me when I hear that sound. I can almost identify it very clearly in today's-- even today. I hear this kind of sound. It gives me the chills. And they walked around immaculately dressed with their pokes. And some of them wore hats, those-- those German hats-- those lovely-- their-- their-- their regalia, so to speak. And how they treated the women. I remember watching, that there was some kind of a counter there in the middle when we went through after we were separated. And I guess, I don't know, we were changed and-- and-- and dressed, how-- how-- I remember this one German. And this woman was naked naturally-- how he made her bend over that counter, whatever it was, and took the poke and poked her in her butt and had somebody look into her butt to see whether she had some jewelry inside her rectum. [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] And how they went around and looked at everybody. And some of us were shaved, some of us were not. It was-- I guess in the life of Auschwitz, it was already at the end of it-- towards the end of it, even though I-- I didn't [? omit ?] the most horrifying killing took place after I came there. The burning and the gassing took-- was night and day after we got there. But still, if you measure it in months, it was towards the end of Auschwitz. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I remember after they-- after they went through us real thoroughly, I-- I felt like I was just an observer there. I was sitting and observing. I was-- it didn't involve me at all. I wasn't part of it. I-- I managed to get myself out of that-- of that place. I wasn't there. And we-- they took us out on the platform, naked, and we stood there for 24 hours, naked on the platform. Evidently, what might have happened, they had no specified place for us. Evidently, what happened is that maybe Mengele wasn't there. Evidently, what happened, they had no-- they had no designs what to do with us. Because we stood there naked for 24 hours on the platform-- just the women. And we were lucky. It was summertime. It was August. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] all of a sudden, we were marched into a-- into a shower, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] and it wasn't the gas chamber. And by that time, already people knew what was happening there. The grown-ups knew. And I could hear things, and it was like rumors. And you don't want to hear it, but you hear it anyhow. But you watch. And you watch people's-- people's reactions and people's-- how they behave themselves. The behavior-- it was incredible. That-- that was my-- my good thing. I-- I watched. Like I said, I took myself out of there as like I was watching from the outside. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] After the showers, [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] it could have been that after the showers, they-- they stood us there for 24 hours. It could have been before. I-- I-- I cannot exactly put it in the proper place, and I'm not going to try. All I know is we-- they marched us into-- into [GERMAN], they called it. I was into numbers always. So we were in block 16 of the [GERMAN] or [GERMAN], they called it. And I remember I was in awe of seeing signs. There were signs posted everywhere, signs. And I didn't know where I learned how to read, but I wanted to learn how to read. And I must have learned it, because I-- I could read them. I could read the signs. And I was fascinated. I was absolutely fascinated by the signs and wanting to read them. And in that block, I don't know if there were, like, 1,600 or 2,000 women in that block where we were put in. And it was triple-- triple-deckers, as you see. And in each section, there were eight women-- I think eight of us. I'm not sure exactly. But we were-- if one turned over, all of us had to turn over. And I remember my mother was with me, and she begged them to put us on the lower bunk, because she knew I-- I-- that I-- that I still wet, I peed. So she-- [LAUGHS] she begged them to put us on the lower bunk, and we were put on the lower bunk. And every night I used to wet the whole thing, everybody, because we were one on top of the other. If one turned, we all had to turn at the same time. It was devastating and embarrassing for me, but I couldn't help it. I just could not help it. I was very lucky that the women didn't-- didn't tell on me, because I knew if they would tell on me I wouldn't be here to tell the story. They all came out of the showers in very sparse clothing. We were given very sparse clothing. We weren't given anything back that we-- that we came with naturally. And we were all deloused, as they called it. We were all sprayed with that powder always to-- because one of the signs read, [SPEAKING GERMAN]. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Or-- or one of the signs read, [SPEAKING GERMAN], which means, one louse, your death. And it had a double-meaning on that. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And I remember we were very sparsely clothed, but it was still summer so we were lucky. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We were in that block not too long. But I remember after we got there, there were French people. There were Hungarian. There were Czechs. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] There might have been some other kind of people, too. But it was a mixed bag, definitely, because I never got to know the-- the people on that side of the divide. There was that holy divide in the-- in the block, which was like a 2 or 3-foot brick raised area in between the block-- in the-- between our-- our block [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] that ran the length of the block. And at each end, there was a chimney going up. At each end of that-- of the block, there was a chimney going up. So on-- on one side of the divide-- I never got to the other one. I was on that side, and I never got to the other side. There were so many people in that block, and I remember there were quite a few from my hometown, like I say. But we were just a little minority because there was so many people in there. And they asked me to sing. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And I figured, listen, if I wet these people every night, maybe I'd better. So I remember getting up to sing. And [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] the block, I--
